title.en: Deploying Claude in the Enterprise: Direct API, Bedrock, Vertex

description.en: Compare the three ways enterprises consume Claude — Anthropic's direct API, AWS Bedrock and Google Cloud Vertex AI — plus Claude for Enterprise for the workforce, and learn a rule-based framework for choosing the channel.

story_variants.en:
## One Model, Three Front Doors

A quirk of the frontier market that surprises many architects: **you can buy the same Claude models through three different channels.** Anthropic sells them directly via its API and console; Amazon resells them inside **AWS Bedrock**; Google resells them inside **Vertex AI Model Garden**. Same model weights, materially different operational wrappers — and the wrapper often matters more to an enterprise than a benchmark point.

This multi-channel reality flows from Anthropic's investor-partnerships with both Amazon and Google, and it hands *you* leverage: procurement, compliance and infrastructure teams each get an option shaped like something they already trust.

## Channel 1: The Direct Anthropic API

Going straight to the source has clear advantages:

- **Newest models and features first.** New Claude releases, beta features (extended thinking modes, prompt-caching improvements, the Files and Batches APIs, MCP-adjacent capabilities) generally land on the direct API before the cloud resellers.
- **The full developer platform**: Anthropic Console with its prompt workbench, evaluation tooling, usage dashboards, and Workspaces for organizing keys and spend limits by team.
- **Simplest mental model**: one vendor, one SDK (first-party Python and TypeScript libraries), one status page.

The trade-offs: it is a *new vendor relationship* — security review, DPA, invoicing — and spend does not draw down your existing cloud commitments.

## Channel 2: Claude on AWS Bedrock

For AWS-centric enterprises, Bedrock's pitch is compelling precisely because it is boring:

- **Procurement is already done.** Claude usage bills through the AWS account and can count toward negotiated enterprise spend commitments.
- **Network and identity fit**: PrivateLink keeps traffic off the public internet, IAM policies govern who may invoke which model, CloudWatch and CloudTrail provide familiar logging and audit.
- **Compliance inheritance**: Bedrock sits inside AWS's certification portfolio, which shortens many security reviews.

Costs of the convenience: new Claude models and API features can arrive **later** than on the direct API, some Anthropic-platform niceties are absent or shaped differently, and you adopt Bedrock's own API conventions and quotas. Regional model availability varies — check before promising a data-residency story.

## Channel 3: Claude on Google Cloud Vertex AI

The mirror image for GCP shops: Claude appears in **Vertex AI Model Garden** alongside Gemini, billed through GCP, governed by Google Cloud IAM, logged in Cloud Logging. Notably, this means a GCP enterprise can run **Claude and Gemini side by side on one platform** — a genuinely convenient setup for the multi-model routing strategies this track keeps advocating. The same caveats as Bedrock apply: feature lag and region-by-region availability.

| Dimension | Direct API | AWS Bedrock | GCP Vertex AI |
|---|---|---|---|
| New models/features | First | Usually later | Usually later |
| Billing | Anthropic invoice | AWS bill / commits | GCP bill / commits |
| Identity & network | API keys, Workspaces | IAM, PrivateLink | Cloud IAM, VPC-SC |
| Best when | Want newest capabilities, no cloud lock | AWS-standardized org | GCP-standardized org |

> **Callout:** Pricing per token tends to be broadly similar across channels, but *discount structures* differ — cloud spend commitments can make a reseller effectively cheaper for your org even at identical list rates. Involve finance early.

## The Workforce Side: Claude for Enterprise

API channels serve your *systems*. Your *people* need a product: **Claude for Enterprise** (and the smaller Team plan) provides the Claude chat experience with SSO/SAML, domain capture, role-based administration, audit logs, an expanded context window for heavy documents, and Projects for shared team knowledge. Two governance facts matter most: enterprise data is **not used for model training by default**, and admin controls decide which integrations (including MCP connectors) employees may attach. Rolling out seats without deciding those settings first is governance-by-accident.

> **Adoption pitfall:** Teams treat the channel decision as permanent and agonize over it. In practice, well-built applications talk to Claude through a thin internal client layer; switching Bedrock→direct or adding Vertex later is days of work, not months — *if* you avoided scattering channel-specific SDK calls across fifty services. Abstract early.

## A Rule-Based Selector

For most organizations the decision compresses into embarrassingly simple rules: **standardized on AWS → Bedrock; standardized on GCP → Vertex; otherwise, or when you need the newest features fastest → direct API.** Edge cases exist (multi-cloud orgs often use the direct API as the neutral option; some run different channels per environment), but the simple rules cover the bulk of real decisions — which is exactly why they belong in code, not in a recurring meeting.

In the challenge you will encode that selector: a function mapping a team's requirements dict to a channel, applied across three mock teams. It is a toy — and it is also precisely the first routing rule of a real enterprise AI gateway.

description.en: The same Claude models reach your workloads through three API channels — plus a seat-based product for the workforce.

left.label: Scatter channel-specific SDK calls across every service

right.label: Route all Claude traffic through one thin internal client layer

description.en: A thin abstraction turns channel switching from a re-platforming project into a config change.

explanation.en: The team's requirements arrive as data — which cloud they are standardized on.
AWS-standardized org: Bedrock inherits IAM, PrivateLink, billing commitments and audit tooling.
GCP-standardized org: Vertex AI — with the bonus of Claude and Gemini side by side.
No strong cloud gravity, or newest-features-first? The direct Anthropic API is the default.

description.en: Three teams, three justified channels — one organization can legitimately use all three at once.
Channel selection as a lightweight, reversible process rather than a one-way procurement door.

instruction.en: Encode the deployment-channel decision as code. Complete choose_channel(req): return 'bedrock' if req['cloud'] is 'aws', 'vertex' if req['cloud'] is 'gcp', and 'direct_api' for anything else (including None). Then, for the three teams in TEAMS in order, print EXACTLY four lines: '<team>: <channel>' for each team, followed by 'channels_used=' plus the number of DISTINCT channels selected. Enterprises really do run all three channels at once — encoding the rule keeps fifty teams consistent without fifty meetings.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
TEAMS = [
    ("team_data", {"cloud": "aws"}),
    ("team_ml", {"cloud": "gcp"}),
    ("team_apps", {"cloud": None}),
]

def choose_channel(req):
    # TODO: "aws" -> "bedrock"
    # TODO: "gcp" -> "vertex"
    # TODO: anything else -> "direct_api"
    pass

# TODO: for each (name, req) in TEAMS, print "<name>: <channel>"
# TODO: print "channels_used=<number of distinct channels>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
team_data: bedrock
team_ml: vertex
team_apps: direct_api
channels_used=3

en: Simple if/elif/else on req["cloud"] — compare against the strings "aws" and "gcp".
Collect each result while printing: channels = [choose_channel(req) for name, req in TEAMS].
Distinct count is len(set(channels)); print it with an f-string: f"channels_used={len(set(channels))}".

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert choose_channel({"cloud": "aws"}) == "bedrock"
assert choose_channel({"cloud": "gcp"}) == "vertex"
assert choose_channel({"cloud": None}) == "direct_api"
assert choose_channel({"cloud": "azure"}) == "direct_api"
assert len(TEAMS) == 3

q.en: Through which three channels can enterprises consume Claude models via API?

en: Direct Anthropic API, AWS Bedrock, and Google Cloud Vertex AI
Direct API, Azure AI Foundry, and Hugging Face

q.en: Which channel generally receives new Claude models and beta features first?
Why might Bedrock be effectively cheaper for an AWS-standardized enterprise even at similar per-token list prices?

en: Claude runs faster on AWS hardware
Usage bills through the AWS account and can draw down negotiated cloud spend commitments

q.en: What is the key architectural practice that keeps the channel decision reversible?

en: Routing all Claude traffic through a thin internal client abstraction instead of scattering SDK calls
Using the same API key across channels
